data.text/html:
Does Attention Need Itself?

Diagnosing wasted attention in GPT-2

See the bright vertical stripe on the left edge? Every token in the sequence is staring at the very first token — the word "The." That stripe is an attention sink. In GPT-2's deepest layers, over 68% of attention goes to one meaningless token. We measured the cost, tested what works and what doesn't, and built a tool to see it.

data.text/markdown:
What is attention?

Every word in a sentence "looks at" every other word to decide which ones matter most. The model splits 100% of its attention budget across all tokens — more attention means "you're important for understanding this position."

What is a sink?

The very first token receives far more attention than it deserves — often 40–60% in deeper layers — regardless of what word it actually is. Researchers call this an attention sink (Xiao et al., 2023; Chen et al., LeCun/NYU).

Why does it matter?

Every percentage point spent on the sink is a point not spent tracking syntax, resolving pronouns, or maintaining long-range context. The model's capacity is finite. The sink wastes it.

data.text/markdown:
Reading the dashboard

Not all low-entropy heads are sick. Some heads should focus narrowly — syntax heads tracking subject-verb agreement, coreference heads linking pronouns to nouns. These show low entropy in every condition. The sink-corrupted heads are the ones with low entropy only in standard attention that recover when you apply a fix. The sink was hijacking their budget. A healthy model has a mix of focused and diffuse heads. The sink corrupts this mix by forcing too many heads to concentrate on the wrong thing.
